title: Illuminators, Lighting, and Composite Viewing
narrationScript: Everything you have learned about making a quality radiograph comes down to a few minutes in a dark room. The exposure was right, the geometry was tight, the processing was clean. None of it matters if the viewing conditions throw that quality away. This lesson is about the viewing station itself. The illuminator, the masking, the room around it, and the special density rules for viewing more than one film at a time. Start with what film density really means for viewing. Density is logarithmic. A density of one passes ten percent of the light that hits the back of the film. A density of two passes one percent. A density of three passes one tenth of one percent. A density of four passes one hundredth of one percent. Every unit of density costs you a factor of ten in light. Code quality radiographs commonly sit between density two and four in the area of interest, so the film you are reading is blocking between ninety nine percent and ninety nine point nine nine percent of the illuminator output. That is why a film illuminator is not a light box from an art store. It is a high intensity instrument sized for the densities you read. Run the numbers and the demand becomes obvious. Industry guidance typically calls for a luminance of roughly thirty candela per square meter coming through the film to your eye, and more is better. To deliver thirty through a density of two, the illuminator must put out about three thousand candela per square meter. Through a density of three, about thirty thousand. Through a density of four, about three hundred thousand. So a general purpose viewer handles the routine range, and a high intensity spot viewer stands by for the dense areas. A good illuminator gives you three more things. Variable intensity, so you can match the light to the film instead of dazzling yourself. Diffused, even light across the whole port, with no hot spots and no visible lamp structure. And cooling, because that much light makes heat, and heat will damage an emulsion left sitting on the port. Masking is the discipline that goes with the hardware. Any bright light that reaches your eye without passing through the area you are reading is glare, and glare resets your vision. Light leaking around the film edges, or blasting through a thin low density strip next to the weld, can be thousands of times brighter than the light coming through the dense area you actually care about, and it will shut down your ability to see low contrast detail. So mask the film edges. Mask low density regions next to the area of interest. Use the viewer aperture or opaque masks cut to the film. If you can see raw illuminator light anywhere, fix that before you read. Now the room. Background lighting should be subdued. Not pitch black, and never bright. There are two reasons. First, light in the room reflects off the film surface toward your eye and lays a veil of brightness over the image, washing out the very contrast you are trying to perceive. No light source should reflect off the film face, and nothing bright should sit in your field of view behind or beside the viewer. Second, your eyes adapt to the brightest thing they see. A bright room keeps your retina set for daylight, and a daylight adapted eye cannot pull faint detail out of a dark film. Give yourself a few minutes in the subdued room before you make calls. We will say more about dark adaptation in the next lesson. Finally, composite viewing. Many techniques load two films in one cassette, and there are two ways to use the pair. In a latitude technique, the films have different speeds and you read each film by itself. The fast film shows the thick part of the range, the slow film shows the thin part, and each film must meet the normal single film density minimum in the area it covers. In composite viewing, you superimpose the films and read them as one. Because density is logarithmic, densities add when films stack. Two films at one point four and one point five read about two point nine together. Typical code rules for composite viewing require each film to reach at least one point three on its own, with the superimposed set meeting a composite minimum of about two point six. And if you ever need to read one film of that set by itself, it must meet the full single film minimum on its own. Get the viewing station right, and every bit of quality you built into the film is still there when it counts.
imagePrompt: A film interpreter in a darkened reading room leans toward a masked weld radiograph glowing on a high-intensity illuminator, dim amber backlight tracing shelves of film envelopes behind him.

sections.heading: Viewing Is Where Sensitivity Is Cashed In
sections.body:
Every choice made in the bay - source, geometry, film class, processing - exists to put information on a sheet of film. The viewing station is where that information either reaches a trained eye or is thrown away. Codes treat viewing conditions as requirements, not comfort items, because the physics is unforgiving. Film density is logarithmic, **D = log10 of incident light over transmitted light**, so a density of 1.0 transmits 10% of the illuminator's light, 2.0 transmits 1%, 3.0 transmits 0.1%, and 4.0 transmits just 0.01%.

Code-quality radiographs typically run between density 2.0 and 4.0 in the area of interest, which means the film you must read is blocking 99% to 99.99% of the light behind it. Reading film is a starved-light task. The 2-2T hole your technique paid to record exists on the film as a density difference of a few hundredths - it reaches your brain only if enough light comes through the film, no stray light dazzles the eye, and the interpreter's vision is adapted to the work. That is the whole agenda of this lesson: the illuminator, the masking, the room, and the density rules that change when two films are viewed as one.

sections.heading: Film-Illuminator Requirements
sections.body:
A film illuminator is a high-intensity instrument sized for the densities it must penetrate. Industry guidance typically calls for a luminance of about **30 candela per square meter transmitted through the film** to the interpreter's eye, and more is preferred - many laboratories aim for 100 or better. Since each density unit cuts the light tenfold, the illuminator must supply 30 x 10 to the power D: about 3,000 cd/m2 to read density 2.0, 30,000 for density 3.0, and 300,000 for density 4.0. That tenfold ladder is why shops keep two classes of viewer: an area or strip illuminator for routine work, and a **high-intensity spot viewer** for dense areas up to 4.0 and beyond.

Four features matter almost as much as raw brightness:

- **Variable intensity** - match the light to each film's density instead of dazzling yourself between a thin film and a dense one.
- **Diffused, even light** across the whole port - no hot spots, no visible lamp filament behind the image.
- **Cooling and heat-absorbing glass** - high-intensity lamps generate serious heat, and an emulsion left on a hot port will be damaged; many spot viewers use a momentary foot switch so full power runs only while you read.
- **Masking provisions** - adjustable apertures and opaque masks, covered next.

sections.caption: Each unit of film density multiplies the illuminator luminance demand tenfold - the reason high-intensity spot viewers exist.

sections.heading: Masking and Edge Glare
sections.body:
Any light that reaches your eye without passing through the area you are reading is **glare**, and glare resets the retina's sensitivity in seconds. Light leaking around the film edges, or pouring through a low-density strip of base metal beside a dense weld image, can be thousands of times brighter than the light transmitted through a density-3.5 area of interest. Under that veil, the few-hundredths density difference that marks a fine crack simply disappears.

The fixes are mechanical and absolute:

- Mask the **film edges** with opaque material so no raw illuminator light escapes around the radiograph.
- Mask **low-density regions** adjacent to the area of interest when you read the dense zones.
- Use the viewer's **adjustable aperture** so only the region being evaluated is illuminated.

The working rule: if you can see unfiltered illuminator light anywhere in your field of view, fix the masking before you make a call. A radiograph evaluated through glare has not really been evaluated.

sections.heading: Background Lighting in the Reading Room
sections.body:
Viewing-room lighting should be **subdued** - not pitch black, never bright. Two failure modes drive the requirement.

First, **reflections**. Room light bouncing off the glossy film surface toward your eye lays a sheet of brightness over the image and erases low-contrast detail. Position lamps so nothing reflects off the film face from the viewing side, and keep bright objects - windows, white walls, other illuminators - out of the field of view behind and beside the viewer.

Second, **adaptation**. The eye sets its sensitivity to the brightest field it sees. A daylight-bright room holds the retina at daylight sensitivity, far too coarse for the faint luminances coming through dense film. Subdued background light, roughly comparable to the luminance of the viewed radiograph, lets the eye settle at the right operating point and stay there, instead of being yanked between extremes every time you glance away. There should still be enough light for safe movement and for reading reports - a fully dark room just trades one adaptation problem for another and invites handling damage.

sections.caption: A compliant viewing station: high-intensity diffused illuminator, masked radiograph, subdued non-reflecting background lighting, and an adapted interpreter.

sections.title: Before you read a single film
sections.body:
- Illuminator can deliver roughly **30 cd/m2 through the densest area** you will evaluate - spot viewer ready for densities near 4.0.
- Intensity **variable**, port light **diffused and even**, lamp heat managed.
- **Film edges and adjacent low-density areas masked** - no raw light in view.
- Background lighting **subdued with no reflections** off the film face.
- Several minutes of **adaptation** in the subdued room.

If any item fails, fix the station - never lower the standard to match the station.

sections.heading: Multiple-Film Loads: Latitude and Composite Viewing
sections.body:
Many procedures load two films per cassette, and there are two distinct ways to use the pair - with different density rules.

- **Latitude technique (separate viewing).** The two films have different speeds. Each is read **alone**: the faster film shows the thick end of the thickness range, the slower film the thin end. Each film must meet the full **single-film** density minimum - typically 1.8 for X-ray exposures, 2.0 for gamma - in the area it is used to interpret.
- **Composite viewing (superimposed).** Two films, usually of equal speed, are exposed so neither meets the single-film minimum alone but the stack does. Because density is a logarithm, superimposed densities **add**: films reading 1.4 and 1.5 view as about 2.9 together. Typical code rules require **each film of a composite set to reach at least 1.3** and the superimposed pair to total **at least 2.6**, with the 4.0 maximum applying to whatever you actually view.

Composite films must come from the **same exposure** and be held in register while viewed. Double loading carries a bonus the artifact lesson will exploit: a true indication prints at the identical location on both films, while most artifacts appear on only one.

sections.caption: Typical density windows for single and composite film viewing, in the style of common codes such as ASME Section V.
rows: Each film of a composite set

figures.title: Illuminator luminance demand versus film density

checkQuestions.question: A weld area reads density 3.0. About what fraction of the illuminator's light reaches your eye through it, and what illuminator luminance is needed to deliver a typical 30 cd/m2 to the eye?
options: 1% transmitted; about 3,000 cd/m2 required
0.1% transmitted; about 30,000 cd/m2 required
0.01% transmitted; about 300,000 cd/m2 required
10% transmitted; about 300 cd/m2 required
checkQuestions.explanation: Density 3.0 means the film passes 10 to the minus 3 of the light - 0.1% - so delivering 30 cd/m2 through it takes 30 x 1,000 = 30,000 cd/m2 from the illuminator.

checkQuestions.question: Why must the film edges and any low-density areas next to the area of interest be masked during viewing?
options: To keep the film flat against the illuminator port
Because unmasked bright light glares into the eye and destroys its ability to see the low-contrast detail in the dense area being read
To protect the film emulsion from illuminator heat
Because codes limit the total area of film that may be illuminated at once
checkQuestions.explanation: Light bypassing the area of interest can be thousands of times brighter than the light coming through it; that glare resets the eye's adaptation and erases the few-hundredths density differences that mark fine discontinuities.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement about background lighting in the film viewing area is correct?
options: The room should be as bright as a shop floor so paperwork can be read easily
The room must be in total darkness at all times
Lighting should be subdued and positioned so it neither reflects off the film surface nor places bright sources in the interpreter's field of view
Background lighting only matters when viewing densities below 1.0
checkQuestions.explanation: Subdued, non-reflecting background light prevents a veil of reflection on the film face and keeps the eye adapted near the film's luminance; total darkness and bright rooms both degrade reading.

checkQuestions.question: Two films from the same cassette read 1.4 and 1.5 and are viewed superimposed. Under typical composite-viewing rules (each film at least 1.3, composite at least 2.6), the set is:
options: Unacceptable - each film must individually reach 1.8
Acceptable - each film exceeds 1.3 and the composite is about 2.9, above the 2.6 minimum
Unacceptable - superimposed densities average to about 1.45
Acceptable only if one film is removed before viewing
checkQuestions.explanation: Superimposed film densities add, so 1.4 + 1.5 gives roughly 2.9 composite; with both films above the 1.3 per-film floor and the total above 2.6, the composite criteria are met.

checkQuestions.question: In a two-film latitude technique, the slower film is used by itself to interpret the thin section of a gamma-exposed part. What minimum density must that film meet there?
options: 1.3, the per-film composite minimum
2.6, the composite minimum
The full single-film gamma minimum, typically 2.0, in the area it is used to interpret
No minimum applies because two films were loaded
checkQuestions.explanation: Whenever a film is viewed alone it must stand on its own: the single-film minimum (typically 2.0 for gamma exposures) applies; the 1.3 floor exists only for films read superimposed as a composite.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiographic Interpretation and Evaluation Course 1.1-1.3
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (film density and viewing requirements, general reference)
Industry illuminator guidance such as ISO 5580 and related ASTM practices (transmitted-luminance expectations, general reference)
